source.title: Efficient production of d-lactate from methane in a lactate-tolerant strain of Methylomonas sp. DH-1 generated by adaptive laboratory evolution

initial_request.research_problem: Lactate toxicity limits high-level lactate production in methanotrophs; prior titers from methane were <1 g/L.
initial_request.known_context: Methylomonas sp. DH-1 is a methanotroph lacking native LDH, suitable for optically pure lactate production. Lactate toxicity is a known bottleneck.
quantifiable_goals: Achieve higher D-lactate titer from methane

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Lactate toxicity limits high-level production; prior titers <1 g/L.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Unknown lactate tolerance of Methylomonas sp. DH-1.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Wild-type Methylomonas sp. DH-1 has low lactate tolerance.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Cell growth severely inhibited; tolerance only up to 0.5 g/L lactate.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Low tolerance motivates adaptive laboratory evolution to improve tolerance.

evidence.quote_or_span: LA toxicity is one of the limiting factors for high-level production of LA.
The cell growth was severely inhibited by addition of LA, exhibiting low tolerance up to 0.5 g/L LA in the medium neutralized to pH 6.8 (Fig. 1b).
we performed adaptive laboratory evolution of Methylomonas sp. DH-1 by serially transferring cells to the medium with increasing concentrations of LA from 0.5 g/L to 8.0 g/L.
DH-1, generated by adaptive laboratory evolution was effective in LA production from methane.
DH-1 generated by adaptive laboratory evolution
DH-1 by adaptive laboratory evolution and generated an efficient D-LA-producing strain by introducing D-specific LDH gene into the evolved strain.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need strains with higher lactate tolerance.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Serial transfer with increasing lactate concentrations will evolve tolerant strains.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Evolved strains JHM30 (tolerant to 3.0 g/L) and JHM80 (tolerant to 8.0 g/L) isolated.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Evolved strains obtained; need to identify genetic basis of tolerance.

evidence.quote_or_span: we performed adaptive laboratory evolution of Methylomonas sp. DH-1 by serially transferring cells to the medium with increasing concentrations of LA from 0.5 g/L to 8.0 g/L.
evolved strains JHM30 and JHM80 were selected, which could survive at 3.0 g/L and 8.0 g/L LA, respectively, during the evolution process.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Evolved strains JHM30 and JHM80 isolated.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Genetic mutations responsible for tolerance unknown.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Whole genome sequencing will reveal mutations associated with tolerance.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 2-bp (TT) deletion in intergenic region upstream of watR operon in both strains; JHM80 also has nonsense mutation in fliE.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Candidate mutations identified; need to validate role of watR.

evidence.quote_or_span: DH-1 and identified that up-regulation of the watR gene due to the promoter mutation is partly responsible for the LA tolerance of JHM80.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: TT deletion in watR promoter identified.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether watR upregulation is responsible for tolerance.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Deletion of watR operon will abolish tolerance.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Delete watR operon genes in JHM80.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Deletion abolished lactate tolerance of JHM80.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Confirms watR role; test if overexpression alone is sufficient.

evidence.quote_or_span: deletion of all three genes abolished the LA tolerance of JHM80, suggesting that the elevated expression of watR is mainly responsible for the LA tolerance of JHM80

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether watR overexpression alone increases tolerance in wild-type.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Overexpression of watR in wild-type will increase lactate tolerance.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Overexpression increased lactate tolerance; OE2 construct failed to recover sensitivity.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: watR validated as key tolerance factor; also test fliE mutation.

evidence.quote_or_span: Overexpression of the watR gene in wild-type strain also led to an increase in LA tolerance.
cells integrated with the OE1 and OE3 expression cassettes showed higher LA tolerance
overexpression of OE2 failed to recover the LA sensitivity of the wild-type strain

evolution_trajectory.state_before: fliE nonsense mutation also found in JHM80.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether fliE mutation contributes to tolerance.
evolution_trajectory.observation: fliE deletion did not improve lactate tolerance of JHM30.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: fliE not responsible; focus on watR for engineering.

evidence.quote_or_span: fliE deletion did not improve the LA tolerance of JHM30

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Tolerant strain JHM80 available; no native LDH.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to introduce D-lactate production pathway.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Heterologous D-LDH expression with glgA deletion will enable D-lactate production.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Integrate D-LDH genes (Lm.LDH, Ld.LDH, Lj1.LDH, Lj3.LDH) into JHM80 chromosome with glgA deletion.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Compare D-lactate production of different LDH variants.

evidence.quote_or_span: 4 heterologous D form-specific LDH genes from L. jensenii (Lj1. LDH and Lj3.LDH), L. delbrueckii subsp. bulgaricus ATCC 11842 (Ld.LDH), L. mesenteroides subsp. mesenteroides ATCC 8293 (Lm.LDH) were introduced into the chromosome of JHM80 while deleting the glgA gene

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which LDH variant yields highest D-lactate.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Lm.LDH and Ld.LDH will be most effective.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Measure D-lactate production in shake flasks.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Lm.LDH and Ld.LDH produced 192 and 187 mg/L D-lactate, respectively.

evidence.quote_or_span: JHM80 strains expressing Lm.LDH and Ld.LDH were more effective in LA production than the strains expressing the other LDH genes, producing 192 mg/L and 187 mg/L LA, respectively

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Production level still low; need to combine tolerance and LDH.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: JHM80 with Lm.LDH and ΔglgA (JHM86) will produce higher titer than wild-type equivalent.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Construct JHM86 and compare with JHM14 (wild-type + Lm.LDH).

tool_or_method.name: Fed-batch fermentation with periodic methane feeding

evolution_trajectory.observation: JHM86 produced 750 mg/L D-lactate after 118 h, 7.5-fold higher than JHM14 (100 mg/L).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Titer improved but still low; optimize process conditions.

evidence.quote_or_span: JHM86 strain produced 750 mg/L D-LA after 118 h
wild-type strain integrated with Lm.LDH (JHM14) showed very low growth rate and produced only 100 mg/L D-LA
Compared with the wild-type strain integrated with the same LDH gene, JHM86 showed about 7.5-fold higher D-LA production, demonstrating the importance of LA tolerance in LA production.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Growth stops early; possible nutrient limitation.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: pH control and extra nitrogen will extend growth and increase titer.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Optimize flask conditions with NaOH and 2× KNO3.
evolution_trajectory.observation: JHM86 produced 1.19 g/L D-lactate at 144 h, yield 0.245 g/g CH4, productivity 0.008 g/L h.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Titer improved but productivity low; identify remaining limitations.

evidence.quote_or_span: JHM86 continued growth up to 120 h, producing 1.19 g/L LA at 144 h with a productivity of 0.008 g/L h
19 g/L D-LA with a yield of 0.245 g/g CH $_{4}$ and productivity of 0.
19 g/L LA with a yield of 0.245 g/g CH₄ and productivity of 0.
6 mM NaOH and 2× KNO₃), JHM86 produced 1.19 g/L LA with a yield of 0.
JHM86 strain produced 750 mg/L D-LA after 118 h

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Optimized titer 1.19 g/L, but growth stops at 96 h without extra nitrogen.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Growth stopped after 96 h likely due to nitrogen depletion; extra nitrogen extended growth to 120 h but productivity remained low (0.008 g/L h).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Nutrient limitation identified; also intracellular toxicity remains.

evidence.quote_or_span: JHM86 strain stopped growth after 96 h, which could be due to the depletion of other nutrients such as nitrogen source
JHM86 continued growth up to 120 h, producing 1.19 g/L LA at 144 h with a productivity of 0.008 g/L h

evolution_trajectory.state_before: JHM80 tolerant to 8 g/L external lactate, but JHM86 growth reduced during production.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Why intracellular production is still toxic.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Intracellular lactate production is toxic despite external tolerance; watR targets unknown.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Intracellular lactate production still toxic; watR target genes and tolerance mechanism remain unknown.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Further elucidation of tolerance mechanisms needed.

evidence.quote_or_span: JHM80 could survive in the presence of 8 g/L LA at pH 6.8, but the JHM80-derived LA-production strain JHM86 showed reduced growth rate although its LA production level was below the maximum tolerance level
suggesting that intracellular production of LA is still toxic in JHM80 strain.
further elucidation of LA tolerance mechanisms and metabolic pathways might be necessary to improve LA production.

validation_methods: Gene deletion and overexpression to confirm watR role
Comparison of JHM86 with wild-type equivalent JHM14
success_verification.final_conclusion: ALE-generated JHM80 with watR upregulation enabled D-lactate production up to 1.19 g/L, the highest reported from methane, but intracellular toxicity and nutrient limitations remain.
limitations: Intracellular lactate production still toxic despite external tolerance
watR target genes and tolerance mechanism unknown
Nutrient depletion limits growth and productivity
